commonMistakes: Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.
Mutating shared data when the task expects a new value or explicit ownership.
Treating browser APIs and language features as the same layer.
Choosing an abstraction from habit rather than required operations.

contentAuthority.canonical: New JS2Next Release 1 curriculum and independently authored content.
contentAuthority.courseTranscripts: Coverage and sequencing research only; never learner-facing authority.
contentAuthority.legacyProjectContent: Supplementary internal reference only; excluded from runtime unless explicitly re-authored and approved.

learningObjectives.text: Explain packages and tooling using a new example and justify the result.
Explain bundling and transpilation using a new example and justify the result.
Explain modules using a new example and justify the result.
Explain imports, exports, and dependencies using a new example and justify the result.

mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Packages and tooling** → **Bundling and transpilation** → **Modules** → **Imports, exports, and dependencies**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Packages and tooling.
choices.label: Synchronous code completes on the current stack. Asynchronous operations begin work that finishes later, allowing the runtime to continue.

choices.label: Packages distribute reusable code and tools. Package managers record dependencies and scripts make workflows repeatable.

choices.feedback: Transfer comes from using the concept with explicit assumptions and checking the resulting behavior, not from reproducing a memorized example.
choices.label: Apply Packages and tooling deliberately, make the relevant inputs and assumptions explicit, and verify the observable result before adding more complexity.

choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from Packages and tooling, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Modern Development Workflow and Modules.
choices.label: Start with a framework abstraction and avoid checking the underlying language behavior.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Modern Development Workflow and Modules.
choices.label: Optimize first, then decide what the code is supposed to do.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Modern Development Workflow and Modules.
choices.label: Copy an unrelated implementation, hide the assumptions, and test only the happy path.

questions.explanation: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
questions.hint: Prefer a sequence that makes failures visible early.
questions.prompt: Which sequence is most reliable when solving a problem involving Modern Development Workflow and Modules?

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Modern Development Workflow and Modules.
choices.label: Use Modern Development Workflow and Modules in every file because more abstraction is always better.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Modern Development Workflow and Modules.
choices.label: Avoid Modern Development Workflow and Modules completely because all abstractions reduce maintainability.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Modern Development Workflow and Modules.
choices.label: Choose based only on line count, without considering readability, correctness, or future change.

choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
choices.label: Use Modern Development Workflow and Modules when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.

questions.explanation: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.
questions.prompt: Which guideline shows the best judgment about when to use Modern Development Workflow and Modules?

sections.body: Packages distribute reusable code and tools. Package managers record dependencies and scripts make workflows repeatable.
sections.title: Packages and tooling

sections.body: Bundlers build dependency graphs and emit deployable assets; transpilers transform syntax for target environments.
sections.title: Bundling and transpilation

sections.body: A module is a file-level unit with explicit imports and exports. It exposes a public surface and makes dependencies visible.
sections.title: Modules

sections.body: Exports define a module's public API; imports declare required values. Narrow APIs and acyclic dependencies improve maintainability.
sections.title: Imports, exports, and dependencies

sections.explanation: The export defines a public API and the import makes the dependency explicit.
sections.title: Worked example

summary: Modern Development Workflow and Modules connects Packages and tooling, Bundling and transpilation, Modules.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.
title: Modern Development Workflow and Modules

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

whyThisMatters: Learners frequently use modern development workflow and modules without a clear model of why it behaves as it does; this lesson makes that model explicit. It belongs to the **Modern JavaScript Development** module.
